title: Prompt Injection and the PVE Defense

questions.question: What is indirect prompt injection?
options: A user typing 'ignore all rules' directly
Instructions embedded in data the agent retrieves (a page, PDF, email, memory note) that override the developer prompt on ingest
A typo in the system prompt
questions.explanation: Greshake et al. coined indirect prompt injection: attacker-controlled retrieved content carries instructions.

questions.question: What does the lesson say processing retrieved prompts is equivalent to?
options: Arbitrary code execution on the agent's tool-use surface
questions.explanation: Greshake's framing: retrieved instructions can hit any tool the agent has access to.

questions.question: Which is NOT one of the five demonstrated exploit classes?
questions.explanation: The five are data theft, worming, persistent memory poisoning, ecosystem contamination, arbitrary tool use.

questions.question: What does PVE stand for?
options: Prompt-Validator-Executor: a cheap fast validator runs on each tool call before the expensive main model commits
questions.explanation: PVE wraps every tool invocation with a cheap validator before main-model commit.

questions.question: Why is 'system prompt says ignore untrusted instructions' insufficient?
options: It is instruction-following, not enforcement; the model can still be overridden by sufficiently persuasive injected content
questions.explanation: Real defense needs source tagging, allowlists, per-step safety, and PVE-style validation, not just prompting.

questions.question: What is overtrust of retrieved memory?
options: Yesterday's agent wrote a poisoned memory note; today's agent reads it and re-executes the injection
questions.explanation: Persistent memory poisoning means injections survive across sessions if memory is treated as trusted.

questions.question: What metadata does the lesson recommend attaching to every piece of content?
options: A source tag: user_message vs tool_output vs retrieved; validator refuses directives in retrieved content
questions.explanation: Provenance tagging lets the validator treat content according to its trust level.
